Getting Started: Initializing an AquaCrop Project

Source: vignettes/settingup.Rmd
settingup.Rmd

Quick Start

The setup workflow is three steps: load, initialize, reload.

1. Load the package

library(aquacropr)

2. Initialize the project

init_aquacrop() creates the full directory structure and downloads the AquaCrop binary in one call. By default (version = NULL), it always fetches the latest officially released binary for your platform — no compiler required:

init_aquacrop(path = "~/my_project")

3. Reload after RStudio switches sessions

Once initialization is complete, RStudio automatically opens a fresh session inside the new project directory. Simply reload the package to continue your work:

library(aquacropr)

You are now ready to write input files and run simulations with run_aquacrop().

Choosing an AquaCrop Version

The version argument gives you full control over which binary is installed.

Latest release (default)

version = NULL always downloads the most recent stable binary published by FAO. This is the recommended option for most users: it requires no compilation and guarantees access to the latest AquaCrop features the moment they are officially released.

init_aquacrop(path = "~/my_project")

Pinned release

Pass an explicit version string when reproducibility matters — for instance, to match a binary used in a published study:

init_aquacrop(path = "~/my_project", version = "7.1")

Note : When init_aquacrop() downloads a binary, it saves it to a local cache directory on your machine. The next time you call init_aquacrop() with the same version, the binary is loaded directly from cache — no internet connection needed, no waiting. The cache is keyed by version string, so "7.1" and "7.2" are stored independently. Switching versions is instant if you have already downloaded them before. The only way to bypass the cache and force a fresh download is to pass force = TRUE.

Development version

version = "dev" compiles AquaCrop directly from the latest main branch source code. Use this when you need features that have not yet made it into an official release. A Fortran compiler is required (see Installing from Source below):

init_aquacrop(path = "~/my_project", version = "dev")

Additional Options

Overwriting an existing project

By default, init_aquacrop() will not touch an existing installation. Use force to reinstall the binary and overwrite to reset the directory structure:

init_aquacrop(path = "~/my_project", force = TRUE, overwrite = TRUE)

Skipping the RStudio project file

An .Rproj file is created at the project root by default. Set use_rproject = FALSE to opt out:

init_aquacrop(path = "~/my_project", use_rproject = FALSE)

Cross-platform projects

The operating system is auto-detected at runtime. You can override this with the os argument — useful when preparing a project on one machine that will run on another:

# Build a Windows-ready project from Linux or macOS
init_aquacrop(path = "~/my_project", os = "windows")

Installing AquaCrop from Source

When version = "dev" is used, init_aquacrop() internally delegates to install_source(), which runs the following steps automatically:

  1. Compiler detection — queries R CMD config FC once and reuses the result throughout. Override with the compiler argument if needed.
  2. Source download — fetches the latest main branch archive from the AquaCrop repository.
  3. Compilation — invokes make with the detected Fortran compiler.
  4. Installation — copies the compiled executable to path.
  5. Cleanup — removes temporary build files unless keep_source = TRUE.

System requirements by platform:

Platform Required tools
Linux GNU Make ≥ 3.82 · gfortran ≥ 6.4.0 or ifort ≥ 18.0.1
macOS GNU Make ≥ 3.82 · gfortran from mac.r-project.org/tools
Windows Rtools

macOS note: The clang compiler bundled with Xcode does not include Fortran support. Always use the gfortran binary from the official R toolchain at mac.r-project.org/tools.

Project Directory Structure

A successful call to init_aquacrop() produces the following layout under path:

my_project/
├── my_project.Rproj      # RStudio project file (if use_rproject = TRUE)
├── aquacrop[.exe]        # AquaCrop executable
├── README.txt            # Project notes
├── CAL/                  # Calendar files (.CAL)
├── CLIMATE/              # Climate input files (.Tnx, .ETo, .PLU, .CLI, .CO2)
├── CROP/                 # Crop parameter files (.CRO)
├── LIST/                 # Project Master files (.PRM)
├── MANAGEMENT/           # Field management files: (.MAN, .IRR)
├── OBS/                  # Observed field data for simulation evaluation
├── OUTP/                 # Simulation output files
├── PARAM/                # AquaCrop program parameter files
├── SIMUL/                # Simulation settings
└── SOIL/                 # Soil profile files (.SOL, .SW0, .GWT)

Relationship to the Official AquaCrop Standalone

Users already familiar with the FAO AquaCrop standalone (plug-in) will recognize the structure immediately. aquacropr preserves all four core directories of the official standalone (LIST, PARAM, SIMUL, OUTP) and extends the design by replacing the historical catch-all DATA/ folder with clearly typed, dedicated directories — one per input category.

Directory AquaCrop Standalone v7.1 aquacropr
LIST/ ✅ Project files (.PRO, .PRM) ✅
SIMUL/ ✅ Simulation settings ✅
PARAM/ ✅ Program parameters ✅
OUTP/ ✅ Output files ✅
OBS/ ✅ Observed field data ✅
CLIMATE/ ❌ (merged in DATA/) ✅ Explicit folder
CROP/ ❌ (merged in DATA/) ✅ Explicit folder
SOIL/ ❌ (merged in DATA/) ✅ Explicit folder
MANAGEMENT/ ❌ (merged in DATA/) ✅ Explicit folder
CAL/ ❌ (merged in DATA/) ✅ Explicit folder

This structure makes large-scale simulation projects easier to navigate, script, and version-control, while remaining fully compatible with the input file formats expected by the AquaCrop executable.

On this page
